Yes—EQ before reverb is useful because it changes what the reverb generates, not just what you hear afterwards. Reasons to EQ before reverb: 1) Prevent reverb from reacting to problem frequencies If the vocal has a resonance at 320 Hz, the reverb will amplify and smear that resonance in time. Cutting it before the reverb can prevent that buildup more effectively than cutting after. 2) Shape the “color” of the reverb Feeding a brighter or darker signal changes the character of the tail. Sometimes you want a darker input but a brighter tail, or vice versa. 3) Reduce sibilance going into reverb Even if you de-ess after, reducing “S” energy before can make the reverb smoother and reduce the need for heavy processing later. 4) Creative effects Band-limited reverb (telephone-style) often sounds more intentional when you filter the send. But: if the dry vocal tone is already right, you can keep insert EQ minimal and use send EQ specifically for the reverb feed. That’s often the best compromise.
If I put an EQ on the send, should it be before or after the send knob? Like does it matter if it’s a plugin on the vocal channel vs on the reverb bus?

Functionally, both can work, but they’re not identical. 1) EQ on the vocal channel before the send - Affects what goes to the send (and potentially what goes to the main output depending on routing). - If it’s an insert on the vocal, it changes the dry vocal too (unless you use a split path). 2) EQ on a dedicated “send” (pre-reverb) Many DAWs let you put plugins directly on an aux track before the reverb plugin. That’s often the cleanest: - Dry vocal stays untouched. - You can tailor the reverb input precisely. 3) EQ on the reverb bus after the reverb Doesn’t change how the reverb algorithm behaves, only the output tone. If you want “what the reverb hears,” put EQ before the reverb plugin (either on the aux track before the reverb, or by sending to a separate pre-EQ aux then into the reverb). If you just want to tone-shape the tail, EQ after. In most cases: - Send EQ (pre-reverb): filter aggressively and remove resonances. - Return EQ (post-reverb): final polish and mix-fit.
Ok. Another thing I’ve been fighting: when I EQ the reverb return with a dip at 300, it sometimes makes the reverb sound hollow or phasey. Is that possible?

It can happen, especially if: - the cut is too deep or too narrow - you’re using a linear-phase EQ with certain settings (can cause pre-ringing, though on reverb it’s usually less obvious) - the reverb already has internal filtering and you’re stacking multiple filters at similar frequencies - you’re doing strong M/S processing that changes the stereo image in a frequency-dependent way How to avoid “hollow” reverb: 1) Use broader, smaller cuts Try -1 to -3 dB with a wider Q (0.7–1.2). Hollow often comes from narrow notches. 2) Prefer dynamic EQ over static Static cuts can make the reverb’s tone unnatural all the time. Dynamic EQ only dips when the mud builds. 3) Adjust the cutoff instead of notching Sometimes a slightly higher HPF (e.g., 220 instead of 180) sounds more natural than a big 300 Hz dip. 4) Check if the reverb has a resonance Some plates have a “ring” frequency. If you notch it too hard, the tail can sound weird. Try moving the dip slightly (e.g., 280 vs 320) and see where it sounds least colored. 5) Consider minimum-phase EQ for reverb returns Often a normal minimum-phase EQ sounds more natural for tone shaping than linear-phase on effects returns.
Good point. I think I was using linear-phase because I heard it’s “better.” So minimum-phase might be better for this?

“Better” depends on the task. Linear-phase preserves phase relationships but can introduce pre-ringing and can sound less natural on transient material. Minimum-phase changes phase but often sounds more musical for broad tone shaping. For a reverb return: - Minimum-phase is usually fine and often preferable. - Linear-phase can be useful for parallel processing where phase alignment matters, but reverb is already a time-smeared signal; the benefits are less clear. So yes: try a standard (minimum-phase) EQ on the reverb bus. If you hear fewer artifacts and it sits better, that’s your answer.
Cool. Now, if I want the vocal to feel “expensive” and glossy, what EQ moves usually help? I know that’s subjective, but are there common patterns?

“Expensive” pop vocal gloss often comes from a combination of controlled low-mids, smooth presence, and air—plus effects that are clean and shaped. Common patterns (with caveats): 1) Control low-mids rather than remove them - Dynamic EQ around 200–400 Hz to prevent buildup on certain notes. This keeps warmth but avoids mud. 2) Presence shaping - A gentle lift or dynamic control around 2–5 kHz can bring clarity. But too much here gets harsh quickly, especially with bright reverbs/delays. 3) Air band - A high shelf around 10–16 kHz can add “sheen.” But if sibilance increases, you’ll need de-essing or dynamic EQ. 4) Sibilance management - De-ess carefully so the top end can be bright without spitty “S” sounds. 5) Effects filtering and level discipline Glossy vocals often have bright delays/reverbs, but they’re filtered and tucked. The reverb might be high-passed a lot, and the delay might be low-passed to avoid clutter. 6) Subtle saturation A small amount of harmonic richness can make the vocal feel more forward and “finished” without needing aggressive EQ boosts. If you want, describe whether your vocal is recorded bright/dark and whether you’re going for modern airy pop or a warmer retro pop vibe.
It’s modern airy pop. Vocal is fairly bright already, so when I boost air it gets sibilant fast. I’m de-essing but it starts to lisp if I push it.

That’s a common issue: bright vocal + air boost + de-esser = lisp risk. Try these alternatives: 1) Use dynamic EQ for air instead of a static shelf Add a high shelf at 12–16 kHz but make it dynamic so it lifts mostly when the vocal is not sibilant. Some dynamic EQs let you set a shelf band dynamically. 2) Split-band de-essing Instead of one broad de-esser, use: - a narrow band focused on the main sibilance (often 6–8 kHz) - optionally a second very gentle one higher (9–12 kHz) if needed This can reduce “spit” without crushing the whole top end. 3) Tame harshness before adding air Sometimes what reads as “sibilance” is actually harshness around 3–5 kHz. If you control that region slightly, you can add air more safely. 4) Use saturation for “air-like” presence Some harmonic exciters add perceived brightness without adding as much sibilant energy as an EQ shelf. Keep it subtle. 5) De-ess the reverb/delay returns Even if the dry vocal is controlled, effects can reintroduce sibilant energy. Filtering or de-essing returns can help you keep the vocal airy without the mix getting spitty.
Great. Back to the original issue: if I had to pick only one place to EQ to fix reverb mud, would you pick EQ on the reverb return or EQ on the send?

If I can pick only one: EQ on the reverb return. It’s the most direct control over what’s actually muddying the mix, and it won’t risk thinning the dry vocal. But if the mud is caused by a specific resonance on the vocal that the reverb exaggerates, send EQ can be more effective. In practice, return EQ is the safer “single move,” and send EQ is the more surgical “preventative” move.
Understood. I’m going to implement: reverb return HPF around 220, dynamic EQ at 320, low-pass around 9k, and ducking with a slower release. Anything else I should check so I don’t chase my tail?

A few sanity checks to keep you from over-tweaking: 1) Gain staging Make sure the reverb return isn’t too loud going into your EQ/compressor. If it’s clipping internally or hitting plugins too hard, it can sound dense and muddy. 2) Reverb mix knob If you’re using it on a send, ensure the reverb plugin is set to 100% wet. Otherwise you’re blending dry signal twice. 3) Mono check Collapse to mono briefly. If the vocal collapses or the reverb gets weirdly loud/quiet, your stereo processing (widening/M/S) might be too extreme. 4) Context listening Make changes with the full mix playing, not solo. Solo reverb often leads to over-filtering. 5) Bypass fatigue After you get something workable, stop and come back later. Reverb decisions are easy to overfit. If after all this it’s still muddy, the next suspects are: the reverb algorithm itself (some plates are inherently thick), the decay being too long for the arrangement, or low-mid buildup in other instruments that needs a small pocket.
Awesome. While I have you: I’m also struggling with general EQ decisions. Like, I don’t know when to cut vs boost, and I end up with a lot of EQ plugins. Any philosophy for EQ in mixing?

A practical philosophy: 1) Start with balance and arrangement EQ is not the first tool for making things fit; fader and pan often solve more than you think. If the vocal is fighting guitars, sometimes turning guitars down 1 dB is better than carving the vocal. 2) Cut to fix problems, boost to add character - Cuts are often for removing resonances, mud, harshness, rumble. - Boosts are for intentional tone shaping (presence, air, weight). But plenty of mixes use boosts to solve problems too; it’s not a strict rule. 3) Use the smallest move that works If you need -8 dB cuts everywhere, something upstream may be wrong (recording tone, masking, over-compression, effects buildup). 4) Prefer wide moves for tone, narrow moves for resonances - Wide Q: “this track is too dark/too thick” - Narrow Q: “this frequency rings” 5) Use dynamic EQ when problems are intermittent If mud only happens on certain notes, dynamic EQ avoids making the whole track thin. 6) Limit EQ stages by assigning roles For example: - Channel EQ: corrective (HPF, resonance control) - Bus EQ: tonal shaping in context - FX return EQ: fit to mix This keeps you from stacking random EQs without a plan.
That “assign roles” idea is helpful. What about EQ before compression vs after compression on vocals?

Both are common; again, different goals. EQ before compression: - Removes low-end rumble so the compressor doesn’t overreact to it. - Tames harsh resonances so the compressor isn’t triggered by them. This often leads to smoother compression. EQ after compression: - Shapes the final tone once dynamics are controlled. - Lets you add presence/air that might have been too spiky pre-compression. A typical vocal chain might be: 1) HPF / corrective EQ (pre) 2) Compressor 3) De-esser (or sometimes before compressor) 4) Tone EQ (post) for presence/air 5) Saturation (optional) 6) Sends to delay/reverb But there’s no single correct order. If your compressor is making the vocal muddy, try cutting low-mids before it. If your compressor makes sibilance worse, de-ess before or use a compressor with a sidechain filter.
I’ve noticed my compressor brings out low-mid thickness when it clamps down. Is that because the high frequencies get reduced more?

Often, yes—compression can change the spectral balance even if it’s not frequency-dependent, because: - Peaks often live in the high-mids (consonants, presence). Compression reduces peaks, which can make the remaining sustained low-mid energy feel louder. - If you compress heavily, the quieter parts (often more low-mid and room tone) get raised. Fixes: 1) Use less compression or a slower attack Let transients through so the vocal keeps clarity. 2) Use a sidechain high-pass filter on the compressor If available, filter the detector so low frequencies don’t drive compression. 3) Pre-compression EQ A small cut in the low-mids before compression can prevent the compressor from emphasizing that thickness. 4) Multiband compression or dynamic EQ Control low-mid only when it blooms.
Great. Any last “quick checklist” for vocal reverb clarity in pop?

Quick checklist: - Reverb send level: as low as you can while still feeling it - Pre-delay: 50–100 ms (tempo-aware) - Decay: shorter in dense sections (often 1.0–1.5 s) - High-pass on reverb: 180–300 Hz - Low-pass on reverb: 7–10 kHz - Dynamic control: dynamic EQ around 250–400 Hz if needed - Ducking: 1–3 dB GR with smooth attack/release - De-ess returns if sibilant - Check routing: delay not overfeeding reverb unless intentional - Mix pocket: small cuts on pads/guitars around 300–400 Hz if masking If you implement those systematically, you’ll usually get a clean, supportive space without mud.
Perfect. I’m going to work through that. If I run into issues again, I’ll come back with specifics like plugin settings and what I’m hearing.
Do that—describe what changes help vs hurt, and whether the problem is worse in verse/chorus, and we can zero in quickly.
